flashcards.Back: A manual routing entry configured using the 'ip route' command. it explicitly defines the destination network, mask, and path.
A manual routing entry configured using the 'ipv6 route' command. it specifies the destination IPv6 prefix and length.
A static route represented as 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 that matches any destination IP. it is used when no more specific match exists.
A static route represented as ::/0 that matches any IPv6 destination. it provides a path for traffic with unknown destinations.
A backup static route configured with a higher administrative distance than the primary dynamic route, ensuring it only appears if the primary fails.
An extremely specific static route targeting a single IP address (mask 255.255.255.255 in IPv4 or /128 in IPv6).
The IP address of the adjacent router interface to which packets should be sent. This is the recommended way to configure static routes on multi-access links.
The local interface through which a router sends packets. for serial point-to-point links, specifying only the exit interface is sufficient.
A static route that only specifies a next-hop IP. The router must perform a second lookup to find the physical exit interface for that IP.
A static route that specifies both the exit interface and the next-hop IP. This is the most efficient configuration for multi-access networks like Ethernet.
The default value for a standard static route is 1, making it more trustworthy than any dynamic routing protocol (except connected routes).
A single static route that represents multiple smaller contiguous subnets, helping to reduce the overall size of the routing table.
When using an IPv6 link-local address as a next hop, you MUST also specify the exit interface, as LLAs are only unique within a single link.
A role played by a floating static route. it 'floats' out of the routing table while the primary route is active and 'floats' back in when it fails.
The global command: ip route [network] [mask] {next-hop-ip | exit-int} [distance].
The mandatory global configuration command required to enable the forwarding of IPv6 packets on a Cisco router.
The Cisco IOS verification command used to view only the manually configured static routes in the IPv6 routing table.
A static route that only specifies the exit interface. The router assumes the destination network is directly connected to that segment.
Setting a static route's administrative distance to 255 makes it unusable, as the router will never install a route with an AD of 255 into the table.
A feature that allows a router to respond to ARP requests for a remote IP if it has a static route via an exit interface on that segment.
Any route in the routing table that could potentially serve as the gateway of last resort, often indicated by the 'S*' code.
A virtual bit-bucket interface. Static routes pointing to Null0 are used to discard traffic for specific networks, often for security or loop prevention.
A logical internal interface. Static routes to a loopback address are often used for management or to provide a stable termination point for services.
A limitation where a static route only provides a path in one direction. return traffic must also have a valid route (static or dynamic) to flow back.
A failure that occurs if the next-hop IP of a static route cannot be reached by any other route in the table, making the static route invalid.
Using a standard public IPv6 address as the next-hop. unlike link-local addresses, specifying the exit interface is optional in this case.
The primary advantage of static routing, allowing the administrator to define exact paths for traffic without protocol-driven changes.
A network that only has a single path to the outside world. Static routing is the most common and efficient choice for stub environments.
The process of using 'ping' and 'traceroute' to ensure that a manually configured path is actually forwarding traffic as intended.
The administrative distance for routes learned via Neighbor Discovery Protocol (RA messages), which defaults to 2.
